State Bias (EG) Compactness Action

Methodology & Math

Understanding redistricting metrics and algorithms.

1. Partisan Bias (Efficiency Gap)

The **Efficiency Gap (EG)** measures partisan asymmetry in seat shares by tallying "wasted votes." A vote is wasted if it is cast for a losing candidate, or if it is cast for a winning candidate above the 50% threshold needed to win. $$EG = \frac{W_D - W_R}{\text{Total Votes}}$$ Values near **0%** represent partisan symmetry. A high negative gap denotes Republican bias; a high positive gap denotes Democratic bias.

2. Mean-Median Difference

The **Mean-Median Difference** compares a party's statewide average district vote share to its median district vote share. If the median is significantly lower than the mean, the party's voters are highly concentrated (packed) into a few districts, diluting their voting efficiency.

3. County Splits

Splits measure how many times counties are divided across multiple districts. Minimizing splits keeps local administrative borders unified, ensuring localized communities of interest share a single, accountable representative.

4. ReCom Spanning-Tree Algorithm

The **ReCom (Recombination)** algorithm is a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) walk that explores redistricting plans.

Step 1: Merge two adjacent districts.
Step 2: Build a spanning tree of blocks.
Step 3: Cut the tree into two equal-population pieces.
Step 4: Check if new shapes satisfy compactness.

This process naturally generates compact shapes and equal-population districts, creating a neutral baseline (ensemble) to evaluate enacted maps.

Disclaimer: All demographic and partisan data used in this application is procedurally generated algorithmically over authentic geographical boundaries for simulation and demonstrative purposes only. Map boundaries & base data: PublicaMundi, OpenPrecincts & U.S. Census Bureau.
Partisan simulations driven by MGGG & GerryChain.
